next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The kitchen had been scrubbed so often the pine boards had gone pale around the hearth, but Della still knew the brick Gabriel Wolfe had struck with the back of his head. It sat just left of the crane hook, darker after rain than the rest, as if the room kept one stain for itself no matter how much lye she spent on it.

She stood in the doorway with cemetery mud drying in the seams of her shoes and listened to the house hold its breath. Ruthie had the coffee pot shoved to the cooler edge of the stove so it would not boil bitter. In the pantry June was penciling dates on a row of peach jars because she could not abide a shelf where the labels leaned crooked. Outside at the pump, Rafe worked the handle hard enough to ring the yard iron, each stroke answered from downriver by the dull concussion of the dam.

Ruthie did not look up from the towel she was folding into exact squares. Her laundress hands were red to the wrists from other people's soap and her own washwater. "If you're fixing to tell it," she said, "do it before the road tells it ugly."

Della set her palm on the back of the nearest chair. The day had left her left thumb thick and mean. "I know."

"Do you?"

Della looked past Ruthie to the dark mouth of the pantry. A peach jar clicked against the shelf, then another. June was trying to be quiet and failing by a glass edge.

"Let her finish in there," Della said.

Ruthie's mouth moved once, not quite a smile. "She has ears either way."

The pump handle stopped. A moment later Rafe came in, face and hair wet from the yard basin, shirt collar dark where...
open_hooks_to_carry: Della forces the grave opening into the present rather than the hearing room alone.
The identification rests on bodily specifics: collarbone, premolar, burial location, and Silas's corroboration.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he and Silas brought him in on a door and lowered him by lantern light because no bell could be rung for that death and no preacher asked. Rain ran over the lid now in narrow red lines. One line found a crack and vanished inside.

Her breath came short again, but not only from the climb. Forty years had sat under that hill and never once forgiven her enough to stay still.

Lucille was saying something about tarps, about morning, about sending for Mr. Hensley and locking the office file. Della heard the words as if through a wall. Rafe bent to snatch up the dropped flagging and stared at the red strip in his muddy hand like he had not known until that moment what color it was.

"No," Della said.

Lucille turned. "No what?"

Della looked at the coffin and then at the slope above it, already shining with fresh seepage. Another blast rolled up from the dam, farther off this time but coming all the same, and a little shower of wet dirt pattered down over the exposed lid.

"No tarp fixes this," she said. "No paper fixes it neither."

Rafe did not argue. That frightened her more than if he had started in shouting. He only looked at the opened bank, his jaw working once before going still.

Silas tucked the cough rag away. "Hill's done the deciding."

Della stood in mud to her ankles with her back afire and her thumb swollen like a club, and knew he was right. The ground had cut past hearing dates and affidavits and all the small delaying tricks by which people pretended time might still be theirs. The fever plot had opened its mouth another handspan. By morning it would want more.
state_deltas_required: Open the fever-plot grave before anyone can close the matter with paperwork or intimidation.
Gabriel Wolfe is publicly identified, collapsing the false solution for Rafe and destabilizing Vernon's hold over the Wolfe inheritance.
